# Code review: Telegram fetcher PoC plan compliance

Scope: repository-local review of the Telegram fetcher implementation against `.github/notes/Telegram fetcher, PoC implementation plan.md`, plus implementation-quality checks where they affect the PoC contract. This review intentionally does not repeat findings already recorded in `.github/reviews/20260517T103458Z.md`.

Verification:
- `go test ./...` passes.
- `go vet ./...` fails on the logger mutex-copy issue noted below.

## Plan Compliance and Implementation Quality

1. **[High] Verbosity and progress levels do not match the PoC contract.**  
   Plan §10.1 defines the default level as lifecycle-only `warn`, `-v` as `info`, `-vv` as `debug`, and `-vvv` as trace / gotd internals. The implementation sets the default level to `Info` and makes the first `-v` jump straight to `Debug` (`internal/log/log.go:45-50`), and the help text documents `-v debug` (`internal/command/root.go:65-66`). Per-page progress is logged at `Info` (`internal/telegram/fetch.go:97-111`, `internal/telegram/fetch.go:216-222`), so large fetches print page-level progress without `-v`, while `-vv` unlocks no separate debug layer.  
   Fix: align the level mapping with the plan (`default=warn`, `-v=info`, `-vv=debug`, `-vvv=trace/gotd`) and move page progress to the intended level.

2. **[High] `go vet` fails because the slog handler copies a mutex by value.**  
   `go vet ./...` reports `assignment copies lock value to clone` at `internal/log/log.go:117` and `internal/log/log.go:126`. `WithAttrs` and `WithGroup` copy the whole handler, including `sync.Mutex`, while the clones still write to the same `io.Writer`. Concurrent logging through grouped/attributed handlers can interleave output, and CI should not accept a vet failure.  
   Fix: move the writer and mutex into a shared pointer state and copy only that pointer when cloning handler metadata.

3. **[Medium] Transport-error backoff and `PEER_FLOOD` guidance are missing.**  
   Plan §10 requires exponential backoff with jitter for transport errors, capped by `--timeout`, and describes RateGuard as the central place for `PEER_FLOOD` fail-fast guidance. `RateGuard.Do` returns `PEER_FLOOD` raw (`internal/telegram/rate.go:32-33`) and returns every non-FLOOD_WAIT error immediately (`internal/telegram/rate.go:35-37`). There is no transient transport classifier, backoff budget, attempt counter, or user-facing `PEER_FLOOD` hint.  
   Fix: add a retry classifier for transient dial/transport/RPC failures, keep it bounded by the command context, and wrap `PEER_FLOOD` with actionable guidance before it reaches the CLI.

4. **[Medium] Fetch summaries cannot report the planned `flood_waits` counter.**  
   Plan §10 and §10.1 define the terminating summary as including `dialogs`, `messages`, `flood_waits`, and elapsed time. The command summaries only include the emitted record count and elapsed time (`internal/command/telegram/fetch.go:112`, `internal/command/telegram/fetch.go:185`), and `RateGuard` does not expose any stats when it sleeps (`internal/telegram/rate.go:61-65`). Users cannot tell whether a run was fast because Telegram had no throttling or slow because retries slept.  
   Fix: thread a small stats collector through fetch calls, increment it in RateGuard, and print `flood_waits=K` in the final summary.

5. **[Medium] Fetch commands do not print the authorized-account lifecycle marker.**  
   Plan §10.1 includes `authorized as @<username> (id=<n>)` in the default lifecycle markers once auth settles. Existing-session fetches only log connection start/finish in `Client.Run` (`internal/telegram/api.go:86-113`); the only code that calls `auth.Client.Status` and logs the user is `Login` (`internal/telegram/auth.go:15-27`). A user with multiple sessions or overridden `--session` paths can start a long fetch without seeing which Telegram account is active.  
   Fix: after `Client.Run` enters the callback, check `auth.Client.Status` once for commands that require authorization and emit the account marker before fetch work begins.

6. **[Medium] `auth status` does not include the active proxy descriptor in status output.**  
   Plan §5.2 says `auth status` prints the active proxy descriptor as `type + host:port` without credentials. The command logs proxy data to stderr through the logger (`internal/command/telegram/auth.go:72-79`), but `PrintStatus` writes only session, authorization, DC, and user to stdout (`internal/telegram/auth.go:72-88`). Under `--quiet`, the proxy line disappears entirely, and scripts parsing `auth status` cannot see whether the run is direct or proxied.  
   Fix: add a credential-free proxy field to `Status` / `PrintStatus` and keep the stderr lifecycle log as secondary diagnostics.

7. **[Low] UID cleanup misses quoted angle-wrapped values.**  
   Plan §4.2 says UID normalization trims surrounding angle brackets, backticks, and quotes. `clean` strips angle brackets before quotes (`internal/telegram/uid/uid.go:95-101`), so an input copied as `"<https://t.me/telegram>"` becomes `<https://t.me/telegram>` after quote stripping and then fails URL parsing. This is exactly the kind of note/shell round-trip the UID grammar is meant to tolerate.  
   Fix: normalize in a small loop, or strip quotes/backticks first and angle brackets second, until no wrapper remains.
